title: Autonomous navigation of intelligent microrobotic swarms in unknown environments - Nature Machine Intelligence
text:
AbstractMicrorobotic swarms, through their collective and reconfigurable behaviours, can flexibly adapt to various targeted delivery and navigation tasks. However, achieving autonomous navigation and obstacle avoidance in unknown environments remains a challenge. Here we propose a reinforcement-learning-based control strategy for swarm navigation under partial observation. To bridge the gap between simulation and reality, we introduce a generalized sim-to-real transfer method, enabling the simulation-trained swarm to effectively explore in unknown environments. Our model combines temporally extended attention with multi-level domain randomization of the environment, perception, actuation and dynamics, allowing the policy to use current sensory inputs and historical context to select magnetic actuation commands. Our model efficiently performs autonomous navigation and obstacle avoidance through benchmarking against human operators in simulated environments. The proposed strategy enables swarm navigation and dynamic obstacle avoidance, cargo transportation, moving target tracking and recovery from temporary vision loss, and hovering by its partial sensing and decision-making. Analysis of action sequences and attention scores reveals that the swarm makes task-priority-based decisions, optimizing trajectories towards the target in unknown environments.

Data availability

No public or pre-existing datasets were used for reinforcement learning training in this study. The Turbo policy was trained in a procedurally generated Gymnasium-based simulation environment. Simulation training trajectories, including observations, actions, rewards and obstacle configurations, are generated online by the simulator and can be reproduced using the training environment and configuration files available via GitHub at https://github.com/seuwanglab/Intelligent-Microrobotic-Swarm-in-Unknown-Environments. The version of the code associated with this publication is available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18153971 (ref. 61). Data supporting the reported results are presented in the article, Extended Data Figs. 1–9 and Supplementary Information, including simulation and experimental performance metrics, trajectory analyses, reward and success-rate statistics, attention-map analyses, ablation results and statistical tests. The data used to generate the attention-map visualizations are provided in the ‘src/attention_maps’ directory of the GitHub repository, including recorded observation data, block-level and head-level attention-weight CSV files, generated attention heatmaps and visualization scripts. The repository also contains training logs, model checkpoints, the trained Turbo policy, the YOLOv5 detection model and deployment scripts used for physical experiments. Reward definitions, training hyperparameters, domain-randomization ranges and statistical summaries are provided in Supplementary Information.
Code availability

The source code used to generate the results in this study is publicly available via GitHub at https://github.com/seuwanglab/Intelligent-Microrobotic-Swarm-in-Unknown-Environments. The specific version of the code corresponding to this publication is available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18153971 and is cited as ref. 61.

Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended dataExtended Data Fig. 1 Key metrics during training.Each panel shows the change of a specific training statistic over training steps, including total loss, policy loss, value loss, entropy, policy/value gradients, transformer block gradients, clipping rate, Kullback-Leibler divergence, advantage mean, and value mean. These metrics reflect the optimization dynamics and stability of Turbo throughout the training process.Extended Data Fig. 2 Swarm navigation and obstacle avoidance in simulated environment.The swarm avoids obstacles and navigates simultaneously, with the green line showing its path. In the presence of dynamic obstacles, the swarm selects an outer route compared to a purely static environment. Upon observing dynamic obstacles, the swarm adjusts its direction with the black arrow.Extended Data Fig. 3 Experimental diagram of human-machine confrontation.Participants use a joystick to control the swarm to navigate and avoid obstacles in different levels. The screen displays the operation interface as well as the results of each competition.Extended Data Fig. 4 Two-dimensional distributions of action direction and target direction for Human and Turbo.The color intensity indicates the frequency of each direction pair. The dashed line represents the ideal consistency (action direction = target direction). The action distribution of Turbo is closer to the ideal consistency.Extended Data Fig. 5 Demonstration of vision loss when the swarm navigates to the target and avoid static obstacles.When t = 127s, the current field of view of the swarm is changed, but the model can quickly adapt its strategy to enable the swarm to continue navigating toward the target location and eventually reach the traget.Extended Data Fig. 6 Attention map of swarm navigation and static obstacle avoidance in simulated environment.During this process, in different blocks, Turbo’s memory length for current obstacle information and historical information varies at different stages, resulting in different global modeling capabilities.Extended Data Fig. 7 Visualization of attention weights in different heads of the last transformer block at various time points during vision loss.Each row corresponds to a distinct attention head, and each column shows a representative time point (t). The intensity of the overlay indicates the magnitude of attention assigned to different regions. The four heads exhibit distinct patterns when attending to historical information. Specifically: Head 1 focuses on short-term environmental recall; Head 2 prioritizes immediate sensory input; Head 3 maintains long-term contextual understanding; and Head 4 enables rapid, instinct-like defensive responses. These specialized attention patterns highlight the diverse roles played by different heads in processing information under vision loss.Extended Data Fig. 8 Neuro-arterial vascular phantom.The photograph shows the vascular phantom used for the biomimetic navigation experiment. The phantom contains branched neuro-arterial channels filled with pig blood and includes a structure mimicking a cerebral aneurysm, providing a vascular geometry for testing autonomous microrobotic swarm navigation.Extended Data Fig. 9 Autonomous navigation of the swarm within a vascular phantom under a biomimetic scenario.The experiment was conducted in a neuro-arterial vascular phantom filled with real pig blood and featuring a cerebral aneurysm structure. The Gray pattern displays the raw imaging view, where the swarm is marked in magenta. The bottom Pseudo-color pattern presents the processed result from Laser Speckle Contrast Imaging (LSCI), which clearly highlights the swarm’s position and morphology (represented by the warm-colored regions). The snapshots illustrate the process of the swarm successfully navigating from its starting position (t = 3s) to a predefined target near the aneurysm (t = 615s, marked by the green circle) under the autonomous control of the Turbo framework.
